Details: Three other treatment technologies were reported effective for 1,4-dioxane degradation at bench- or pilot-scale studies, including gamma irradiation, corona discharge, photocatalysis, and sonication (sonolysis). A gamma irradiation dose of 750 Gy decomposed 1,4-dioxane by more than 80 percent with an initial concentration of 10 mg/L [2100]. Corona discharge, an emerging advanced oxidation process, was reported to effectively degrade 1,4-dioxane from 18 ug/L by greater than 90 percent in a pilot study [2102]. Sonication was found effective for 1,4-dioxane removal depending on contact time, frequency, and power. For 100 mg/L of 1,4-dioxane, a degradation efficiency of 78 percent was achieved in 200 minutes with an ultrasonic frequency of 20 kHz and 300W of power [2032]. A study using a continuous flow reactor and the same frequency (20kHz), however, resulted in minimal degradation (12 percent) [2724]. Ultrasonic frequencies and sparging gas were reported to affect the destruction efficiency [2090, 2036]. Catalyst (titanium dioxide) and oxidants (ferrous, iron and persulfate) generally improve the overall degradation efficiency [2082, 2032]. Combination of sonication with photocatalysis (UV + TiO2) generated significantly higher degradation efficiencies [2035]. Photocatalysis using visible light and TiO2 alone has shown to be effective at degrading 1,4-dioxane in bench-scale studies (up to >99 percent removal) [2723, 2726, 2728]. These technologies may not be suitable for full-scale drinking water treatment. Additionally, one bench-scale study found titanium dioxide alone ineffective for removing 1,4-dioxane (2 percent removal) [2055].
